title: Gooseberry jam
subtitle: Tart early-summer jam with a natural high pectin set.
excerpt: Gooseberries have enough natural pectin to set a firm jam without added pectin. Slightly underripe fruit works best: the pectin content is highest before full ripeness. Tart, vivid and a long way from anything commercial. Makes about 4 jars.
sourceNotes: Gooseberry jam. Gooseberries have been cultivated in Britain since the 16th century and gooseberry jam appears in British preserving manuals throughout the 19th and 20th centuries. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) contains several gooseberry preserve recipes.

content.text: Gooseberries are one of the best jam fruits: high in pectin, high in acid, and sharp enough to balance the large amount of sugar required. Use slightly underripe gooseberries if possible; they give a firmer set and more vivid colour. Top and tail each berry before cooking.
Cook the fruit and add sugar

Put {{gooseberries}} of prepared gooseberries and {{water}} of water in a jam pan. Bring to a simmer and cook for 10 to 15 minutes, stirring, until the fruit is completely soft and broken down. Add {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar and stir over low heat until fully dissolved.
Boil to setting point and jar

Bring to a full rolling boil and cook hard for 8 to 12 minutes. Test with a cold saucer: a drop pushed with a finger should wrinkle and hold its shape. On a thermometer: 104.5°C. Skim any foam from the surface. Ladle into sterilised jars and seal immediately.

items.fix: Return to the boil and test again every 5 minutes. Very ripe gooseberries may need longer; add the juice of a lemon for extra pectin.
items.cause: Did not reach setting point; or very ripe berries with lower pectin.

items.fix: Gooseberries set quickly; test early. A slightly over-set batch is still edible but firm.
items.cause: Boiled too long past setting point.

items.fix: Soften the fruit thoroughly before sugar goes in. Once the sugar is in, keep the heat high; long slow cooking after sugar causes darkening.
items.cause: Sugar added before fruit was softened, or fruit overcooked.
items.symptom: Dark, toffee-coloured jam instead of vivid green or pink

content.text: Gooseberries are among the most underrated British fruits, with a short season in June and July. Gooseberry jam preserves the tartness that fresh gooseberries possess in a form that lasts all year. Spread on white bread with butter, or used to fill a Victoria sponge instead of strawberry jam, it is a more interesting preserve than most commercial alternatives.
